meta.note: 每条摘要是官方 Guide 的精选内容。用户点击原文链接跳转到 guide.cryosparc.com 查看完整内容。这是合法引用，非全文复制。

import_movies.description: Import Movies allows you to import raw movie frames into cryoSPARC for downstream motion correction.
outputs: Imported movies ready for motion correction

parameters.name: Pixel size (A)
parameters.official_desc: The physical pixel size of the detector at the specimen level. For super-resolution mode, divide the super-resolution pixel size by 2.
parameters.notes: Critical parameter — errors here propagate to all downstream jobs (CTF, reconstruction, resolution)

parameters.name: Accelerating voltage (kV)
parameters.official_desc: The accelerating voltage of the microscope, typically 200 or 300 kV.
parameters.notes: Must match actual microscope settings

parameters.name: Spherical aberration (mm)
parameters.official_desc: The spherical aberration coefficient (Cs) of the objective lens.
parameters.notes: Titan Krios typically 2.7 mm; 200kV scopes typically 2.0 mm

parameters.name: Total exposure dose (e⁻/Å²)
parameters.official_desc: The total electron dose used during data collection.
parameters.notes: Typical values: 40-70 e⁻/Å² for single-particle cryo-EM

warnings: Incorrect pixel size is one of the most common errors in cryo-EM processing
Ensure you are importing raw movie frames, not already motion-corrected micrographs

patch_motion.description: Patch Motion Correction performs local (patch-based) alignment of movie frames to correct for beam-induced motion.
warnings: Excessive drift may indicate sample stability issues — not fixable by software parameters alone

patch_ctf.description: Patch CTF Estimation determines the contrast transfer function parameters for each micrograph by fitting the power spectrum of local patches.

parameters.name: Amplitude contrast
parameters.official_desc: The fraction of total contrast attributed to amplitude contrast (vs phase contrast).
parameters.notes: Typical values: 0.07 for 300kV, 0.1 for 200kV

parameters.name: Defocus search range
parameters.official_desc: The range of defocus values to search during CTF fitting.
parameters.notes: Wider range increases chance of finding correct values but increases computation

warnings: Poor CTF fits should be filtered out using Curate Exposures before particle picking
Defocus search range too narrow may miss the true defocus value

blob_picker.description: Blob Picker automatically picks particles by band-pass filtering and finding local maxima, without requiring templates or training data.

parameters.name: Use circular, elliptical, or ring blob
parameters.official_desc: Blob picker can generate circular blobs (best for generally globular targets), elliptical blobs (best for rod or disk shaped targets), or ring blobs (best for hollow targets).
parameters.notes: We recommend only one mode per individual Blob Picker job due to differential normalization between blob types.
parameters.version_changes: v5: Between 2 and 10 circular blobs generated (before v5: always 3)

parameters.name: Minimum / Maximum particle diameter (A)
parameters.official_desc: Blobs are generated with sizes varying in one dimension from min to max diameter.
parameters.notes: Exact behavior differs between circular/elliptical/ring modes and between v5 vs earlier versions. Measure protein diameter in PyMOL or ChimeraX.
parameters.version_changes: v5: Blob size spacing parameter added

warnings: Blob picking is fast but generally the least accurate method
Incorrect particles can be filtered using 2D Classification and Select 2D

template_picker.description: Template Picker uses 3D projections as templates for cross-correlation based particle picking. More accurate than Blob Picker when good templates are available.

parameters.name: Particle diameter (A)
parameters.official_desc: The expected diameter of the particle, used to limit the search area.
parameters.notes: Use the same diameter as Blob Picker for consistency

warnings: Template Picker quality depends heavily on the quality of the input templates
Can be used to fill in orientations missed by Blob or Topaz picking

topaz_picker.description: Topaz uses deep learning (U-Net) trained on manually picked particles to automatically identify particles in micrographs. More accurate than Blob Picker, especially for low-contrast data.
inputs: Training micrographs with known particle coordinates

parameters.name: Expected number of particles
parameters.official_desc: The approximate number of particles expected per micrograph.
parameters.notes: Adjust based on particle density — higher for dense samples, lower for sparse

warnings: Overfitting: training loss decreases but validation loss increases — need more/varied training data
Topaz training requires good initial particle picks as ground truth

extract.description: Extract From Micrographs cuts out particle images from micrographs based on coordinate files.

parameters.name: Extraction box size (pix)
parameters.official_desc: The size of the box in pixels to extract around each particle. Should be large enough to contain the entire particle with margins.
parameters.notes: Use box sizes from the efficient sizes list: 32, 36, 40, …, 64, 72, …, 256, 320, 384, 448, 512, 576, 640, 1280

parameters.name: Fourier crop to box size (pix)
parameters.official_desc: If set, particles are Fourier-cropped to this box size (binning). Smaller values speed up computation at the cost of resolution.
parameters.notes: For initial screening: crop to ~100-120px. For final refinement: disable crop.

warnings: Box size too small → particles truncated, cannot align properly
Box size not from efficient sizes list → FFT performance degraded

2d_classification.description: 2D Classification groups particles by similarity and produces class average images. Used to identify and remove junk particles before 3D reconstruction.

parameters.name: Number of 2D classes
parameters.official_desc: The number of classes to divide particles into. 50-200 is a good starting point depending on particle count.
parameters.notes: Rule of thumb: >200k particles → 100 classes; 50k-200k → 50; <50k → 20-30

parameters.name: Force max over poses/shifts
parameters.official_desc: When ON, forces alignment by taking the maximum over poses. When OFF, marginalizes over poses — better for low-SNR but ~5-6x slower.
parameters.notes: Turn OFF for small particles (<100 kDa) or low SNR data to reduce stripe noise

parameters.name: Online-EM iterations
parameters.official_desc: Number of iterations for the online expectation-maximization algorithm.
parameters.notes: More iterations (40-80) give better convergence at cost of computation time

parameters.name: Batchsize per class
parameters.official_desc: Number of particles used to update each class average per iteration.
parameters.notes: Larger batchsize (200-500) provides more information per update but uses more memory

warnings: Stripe noise / pure noise classes → turn OFF Force max over poses/shifts, increase iterations
Too few classes → junk mixed into good classes. Too many → signal dilution per class
For small particles (<100 kDa), consider skipping 2D and using Heterogeneous Refinement directly (~3x more good particles retained)

ab_initio.description: Ab-Initio Reconstruction generates initial 3D density maps from 2D particles without requiring any prior structural information, using stochastic gradient descent (SGD).

parameters.name: Number of Ab-Initio classes
parameters.official_desc: Number of 3D classes to reconstruct. 3 is standard.
parameters.notes: More classes help capture heterogeneity but increase computation

parameters.name: Class similarity
parameters.official_desc: Controls how similar output classes can be. Lower values force classes to be more distinct.
parameters.notes: Default 0.1; use 0.03 to force greater separation between classes

warnings: Always run Remove Duplicate Particles before Ab-Initio to avoid overfitting artifacts
Symmetry must be specified correctly — incorrect symmetry degrades reconstruction

heterogeneous.description: Heterogeneous Refinement performs 3D classification by refining multiple 3D references simultaneously and assigning particles to the best-matching class.
inputs: Particles + all Ab-Initio volumes (good and bad)

parameters.name: Refinement box size (Voxels)
parameters.official_desc: Size of the box in voxels for refinement. Smaller is faster on GPU.
parameters.notes: Same as extraction box size for consistency

warnings: Input ALL Ab-Initio classes (good and bad) for effective heterogeneity separation
Only one class is typically selected for downstream high-resolution refinement

homogeneous.description: Homogeneous Refinement performs high-resolution 3D refinement assuming a single conformation, using gold-standard FSC, per-particle CTF correction, and optional Ewald sphere correction.
outputs: Half maps A+B, Full map, FSC curve, alignments3D
warnings: Gold-standard FSC 0.143 criterion determines reported resolution
Follow with Non-uniform Refinement for optimal final resolution

non_uniform.description: Non-uniform Refinement is an advanced refinement method that uses data-driven per-frequency weights instead of a single B-factor for sharpening, producing optimal final density maps especially for flexible regions.
inputs: Best class from Homogeneous + volume + mask
outputs: Final refined map with local resolution estimation

parameters.name: Dynamic mask use absolute value
parameters.official_desc: When OFF, the algorithm automatically computes a soft mask excluding solvent noise.
parameters.notes: Keep OFF for standard processing

warnings: This is the final push step for resolution — essential after Homogeneous Refinement
Per-frequency weighting replaces traditional B-factor sharpening
